content.text: When an enterprise routes thousands of production requests per hour through a third-party LLM API, the vendor's reliability guarantees stop being abstract contract clauses and become direct drivers of customer experience, revenue, and operational risk. LLM vendor SLA monitoring is the engineering discipline of continuously measuring whether a provider is meeting its contractual obligations on uptime, latency, error rate, and throughput—then automatically detecting breaches, calculating owed service credits, and generating the evidence needed to collect them. Done well, it transforms an SLA from a document filed and forgotten into a live performance feedback loop that keeps vendors accountable, informs capacity planning, and recovers real money when service degrades.
What It Is

LLM vendor SLA monitoring is an automated observability practice that tracks the quality-of-service dimensions spelled out in a vendor's Service Level Agreement and raises alerts—or initiates remediation—whenever measured values drift outside contractual bounds. Unlike general application monitoring, which focuses on your own system's health, SLA monitoring treats the vendor API as an external dependency whose behavior must be continuously verified against legally binding thresholds.

A typical LLM SLA covers four major dimensions. First, API availability (uptime): the percentage of time the endpoint returns any response at all, usually expressed as a monthly rolling figure (99.9% permits 43.8 minutes of downtime per month; 99.99% permits only 4.4 minutes). Second, latency: contractual guarantees at specific percentiles—commonly p50 below 500 ms, p95 below 2,000 ms, and p99 below 5,000 ms for standard completion requests. Third, error rate: the fraction of requests that return 5xx server errors or timeout, often capped at 0.1% of total traffic. Fourth, throughput: tokens-per-minute or requests-per-minute throughput tiers that the vendor commits to serving without artificial throttling beyond agreed rate limits.

Monitoring these dimensions requires two complementary data streams. Synthetic probes make scheduled API calls with predictable payloads every 60 seconds, independent of real user traffic, to maintain a continuous availability and latency baseline. Real-traffic instrumentation samples actual production requests, capturing the full latency distribution under genuine load profiles including prompt length variation, streaming vs. non-streaming calls, and concurrent request patterns. Together they provide both a synthetic floor measurement and a load-realistic view of vendor performance.

The output of SLA monitoring is not just dashboards—it is actionable evidence. When a breach occurs, the monitoring system must produce timestamped records, error logs, latency samples, and uptime calculations in a format the vendor's claims process accepts. Without automated collection and retention of this evidence, enterprises often discover they have experienced an SLA breach but cannot prove it precisely enough to recover credits. SLA monitoring closes that gap.
Why It Matters

LLM APIs now sit on the critical path of revenue-generating workflows. A customer support chatbot that goes dark during an outage leaves users without help. A code-review assistant that spikes to 10-second latency slows developer velocity. A document-processing pipeline that begins returning 500 errors silently corrupts downstream data. In each case, vendor degradation directly translates into business impact—but without monitoring, the connection between the vendor's failure and your business metric remains invisible.

Financial exposure is the most concrete argument. Enterprise LLM contracts commonly specify service credits of 10–30% of monthly spend for each tier of availability breach. At $200,000 per month in API spend, a single month where the vendor delivers 99.5% uptime instead of the promised 99.9% could entitle your organization to $20,000–$60,000 in credits. Across multiple vendors and 18 months, unclaimed credits routinely exceed seven figures. Automated monitoring is the only practical way to catch every breach and file every claim.

Vendor SLA monitoring also provides the data needed to make multi-vendor routing decisions intelligently. When you can compare real-time p95 latency across OpenAI, Anthropic, and a self-hosted model simultaneously, you can route latency-sensitive workloads to whichever vendor is performing best at that moment. This routing intelligence is only as good as the measurement system feeding it. Monitoring is therefore not just a compliance activity—it is core infrastructure for cost-optimized, resilient AI systems.

Finally, SLA monitoring provides the objective data needed for vendor negotiations. When renewing contracts, organizations that arrive with 18 months of detailed performance records are in a fundamentally stronger negotiating position than those relying on subjective recollections. Historical latency trends, documented outages, and breach frequency statistics transform a renewal conversation from a vendor pitch into a performance review grounded in evidence.
Benefits
A mature SLA monitoring program delivers benefits across financial, operational, and strategic dimensions:

Automatic credit recovery: every SLA breach is detected with timestamped evidence, and credit claim amounts are computed automatically—eliminating manual audit work and ensuring no breach goes unclaimed.

Vendor accountability: when vendors know you are monitoring precisely, they treat your account differently. Support escalations backed by monitoring data are resolved faster and escalated higher than vague complaints.

Faster incident response: monitoring systems detect vendor degradation seconds after it begins, allowing on-call engineers to initiate fallback routing or customer communications before users report problems.
Multi-vendor routing optimization: real-time latency and error rate data across vendors enables dynamic routing that favors the best-performing provider at any given moment.
Capacity and cost planning: historical throughput data reveals which vendor tiers are being underutilized and which are consistently hitting rate limits, informing contract tier adjustments.
Contract negotiation leverage: 18 months of objective performance records give procurement teams precise data to demand better SLA terms or lower prices at renewal.

Incident correlation: linking vendor outages to your own service incidents closes the attribution gap—your post-mortem can state precisely that the vendor caused 87% of the customer-visible errors during a window.
Regulatory and audit readiness: industries with uptime requirements (financial services, healthcare) can demonstrate vendor risk management with exportable monthly SLA reports.
Reduced business continuity risk: sustained monitoring of latency trends often reveals gradual degradation weeks before an outage, allowing proactive capacity adjustments.
Engineering confidence: product teams gain trust in the LLM infrastructure when they can see its live reliability metrics, reducing over-engineering of defensive layers.
Trade-offs and Challenges

Building and operating a vendor SLA monitoring system introduces its own operational complexity and cost. Synthetic probes that fire every 60 seconds per vendor generate API usage that you pay for. For a model charged at $0.01 per 1,000 tokens, a probe sending 200-token requests to three vendors adds roughly $26 per month per vendor in monitoring overhead—small compared to production spend but not zero, and it must be budgeted explicitly.

False positives are a significant operational hazard. If a network hiccup between your monitoring agent and the vendor causes a single probe to time out, you do not want a P1 alert firing and an engineer waking at 2 AM. This requires a confirmation window—typically requiring two or three consecutive probe failures within a short window before declaring an outage—which introduces a lag between the actual start of a vendor problem and when your system declares it. You must tune this trade-off carefully based on your business's sensitivity to false alarms versus detection latency.

SLA contract language introduces another challenge. Many vendors define uptime in ways that exclude planned maintenance windows, certain error categories, or issues caused by customer-side behavior (such as malformed requests triggering 4xx errors). Your monitoring system must implement the same exclusion logic as the contract, or you will calculate breach amounts that the vendor will legitimately dispute. This requires careful legal and technical alignment when implementing the breach detection logic.

LLM-specific monitoring presents unique challenges not found in traditional API monitoring. LLM responses are probabilistic—there is no easy way to assert that the response content is correct, only that it arrived and had the expected structure. Streaming responses complicate latency measurement because time-to-first-token, inter-token latency, and total completion time are three distinct metrics, each with different SLA relevance. Partial responses—where the API returns 200 OK but cuts off the completion mid-sentence—are a category of quality failure that standard HTTP monitoring tools do not detect.
Monitoring infrastructure cost: time-series databases, alert managers, and Grafana instances add operational overhead that must be staffed and maintained.
Contract interpretation complexity: mapping contract language to metric thresholds requires legal and engineering collaboration, and contracts differ substantially between vendors.
Evidence collection gaps: if your monitoring system has its own downtime during a vendor outage, you may lose the evidence needed to prove the breach—requiring redundant monitoring infrastructure.
Claim filing friction: many vendors still require manual claim submission via support tickets, reducing the value of automated breach detection unless you also automate the filing workflow.
Metric alignment disputes: vendors often publish their own uptime metrics on status pages that differ from your measurements, leading to disputes that require escalation to resolve.
Streaming latency complexity: TTFT, inter-token latency, and total latency require separate instrumentation and may each be covered by different SLA clauses.
Rate limit noise: 429 errors from hitting your own rate limits must be distinguished from 429 errors caused by vendor-side capacity constraints to avoid misclassifying breach evidence.
How It Works

SLA monitoring operates as a pipeline with four stages: data collection, metric computation, breach detection, and response. Understanding each stage and how they interconnect is essential for building a system that is both accurate and actionable.

Data collection has two independent channels running concurrently. Synthetic probes are small programs deployed independently of your application that make scripted API calls to each vendor on a fixed schedule—typically every 60 seconds. Each probe sends a standardized request (same prompt, same parameters) and records the full timing breakdown: connection establishment time, time-to-first-token for streaming APIs, total completion time, HTTP status code, and response body length. Probes run from multiple geographic regions to distinguish local network issues from genuine vendor outages. Real-traffic instrumentation wraps your production API client with a thin interceptor that records the same metrics for a configurable sample of actual requests—often 10–20% of traffic—without adding latency to the hot path.

Metric computation runs continuously over the collected data. Latency percentiles (p50, p95, p99) are computed using efficient streaming algorithms such as t-digest or HDRHistogram across rolling time windows—commonly 1 hour, 24 hours, and 7 days. Rolling 30-day uptime is calculated as (total minutes - downtime minutes) / total minutes × 100, where downtime minutes are windows in which the vendor returned more than a configured threshold of errors or failed to respond to probes. Error rates are computed separately for 4xx and 5xx responses, with special handling for 429 (rate limit) codes that may or may not count against the SLA depending on contract terms.

Breach detection compares computed metrics against SLA thresholds using a state machine with hysteresis to prevent flapping. When a metric exceeds its threshold, the system enters a confirmation period—typically 3 to 5 minutes—during which it continues to check. If the metric remains above threshold for the entire confirmation period, a breach is declared, the start timestamp is recorded, and alerts are fired. The breach state persists until the metric returns to within-SLA bounds for a recovery confirmation period, at which point the end timestamp is recorded and the total breach duration is logged. This double-confirmation approach reduces false positives while keeping detection latency under 10 minutes for genuine outages.

The response layer handles the downstream actions triggered by breach events. Immediate actions include PagerDuty incident creation, Slack notifications to the infrastructure channel, and if automated fallback routing is configured, switching production traffic to an alternative vendor. Deferred actions include generating the breach evidence package (timestamped metric samples, probe logs, error rate data) and, if the vendor provides an API for it, automatically submitting a credit claim. Credit calculation uses the breach duration, affected traffic volume, and the credit formula from the contract to compute the claim amount, storing this in a credit ledger for reconciliation against future invoices.
Architecture and Design

A production-grade LLM SLA monitoring architecture separates concerns across three tiers: data ingestion, processing, and presentation. Each tier scales independently and can tolerate failures in other tiers without losing the primary monitoring function.

attrs.alt: LLM Vendor SLA Monitoring Architecture: data collection (synthetic probes, real traffic, status pages) flows into a metrics store, then through the SLA engine and alert manager to dashboards and credit tracker

content.text: The ingestion tier consists of three data sources. Synthetic probe agents run as lightweight containers (or Lambda functions) in multiple availability zones, completely independent of the application being monitored. Each probe agent is responsible for one vendor endpoint and writes results to a time-series database over a local push gateway. Real-traffic interceptors run as in-process middleware in your application servers, sampling requests according to a configurable rate and writing sampled observations asynchronously to a queue (Kafka or SQS) to avoid adding latency to the hot path. Vendor status page scrapers poll official incident feeds—most vendors publish Atom or JSON feeds—every 60 seconds, ingesting planned maintenance windows that must be excluded from breach calculations.

The processing tier centers on Prometheus for short-term metric storage (90 days) and Thanos or Cortex for long-term storage (18+ months) needed for annual SLA reporting. Recording rules pre-compute rolling percentiles and uptime calculations to keep dashboard query latency below one second. The SLA engine runs as a Python or Go service that consumes Prometheus metrics via the HTTP API, applies the breach detection state machine, writes breach events to a PostgreSQL breach log, computes credit amounts, and publishes alert events to a message queue consumed by the alert manager. Planned maintenance windows from the vendor feed are loaded into a maintenance schedule table that the breach calculation queries to exclude any downtime that falls within a declared window.

The presentation tier consists of Grafana dashboards organized into three layers. The executive SLA scorecard provides a single-page view of each vendor's current month uptime, latency compliance rate, error rate, and accumulated credit balance—designed for a 30-second read by a non-technical stakeholder. The operational dashboard shows real-time latency time series, error rate trends, and probe health across all vendors. The incident investigation view provides drill-down into specific breach events with minute-by-minute probe results, error breakdowns, and credit calculation details for claim filing.
When to Use It

SLA monitoring is worth the investment once your LLM API spend crosses a threshold where potential credits from breach recovery would offset the cost of building and operating the monitoring system. A rough rule of thumb: if your monthly API spend exceeds $10,000 across one or more vendors, the risk-adjusted expected value of credit recovery typically justifies the investment. Below this threshold, manual monitoring with vendor status pages may be sufficient.
When LLM APIs are on the critical path of revenue-generating user flows—any degradation directly reduces conversion, engagement, or output quality.
When using two or more LLM vendors and making routing decisions—monitoring provides the real-time signal needed to route intelligently.
When operating in regulated industries (financial services, healthcare) that require documented evidence of vendor risk management.
When negotiating enterprise contracts with SLA terms—monitoring proves ROI and provides negotiation leverage at renewal time.
When your SLA includes latency tiers with credit triggers—uptime-only monitoring misses latency breaches that may be more frequent and financially significant.
When your engineering team has experienced vendor outages that were only discovered via user complaints—synthetic probes detect outages before users do.
When monthly API spending approaches or exceeds $50,000—credit recovery potential becomes substantial relative to monitoring operational costs.
When preparing for a QBR (quarterly business review) with a vendor—performance data transforms the meeting from a vendor pitch into an accountable review.
How to Implement

The following Python class implements the core SLA monitoring logic: Prometheus metric registration, latency tracking with p50/p95/p99 computation, error rate monitoring with error classification, uptime tracking, breach detection with confirmation windows, and automatic credit calculation based on breach duration and contract terms.

import time
import threading
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from typing import Optional
from prometheus_client import Histogram, Counter, Gauge, start_http_server
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from collections import deque
import statistics

@dataclass
class SLAConfig:
    vendor: str
    uptime_sla: float = 99.9          # minimum monthly uptime %
    p95_latency_sla_ms: float = 2000  # p95 latency threshold in ms
    p99_latency_sla_ms: float = 5000  # p99 latency threshold in ms
    error_rate_sla: float = 0.001     # max error rate (0.1%)
    monthly_spend_usd: float = 0.0    # for credit calculation
    credit_pct_per_breach: float = 0.10  # 10% credit per breach window
    breach_confirm_window_sec: int = 300  # 5 min confirmation period

@dataclass
class BreachEvent:
    vendor: str
    breach_type: str          # 'uptime', 'latency_p95', 'latency_p99', 'error_rate'
    start_ts: datetime
    end_ts: Optional[datetime] = None
    credit_usd: float = 0.0
    claimed: bool = False

class LLMSLAMonitor:
    def __init__(self, config: SLAConfig):
        self.config = config
        self.v = config.vendor
        self._latency_window: deque = deque(maxlen=10000)  # rolling 1h samples
        self._error_counts = {"4xx": 0, "5xx": 0, "429": 0, "timeout": 0}
        self._total_requests = 0
        self._breach_candidates: dict[str, Optional[float]] = {}  # type -> candidate_start
        self._active_breaches: dict[str, BreachEvent] = {}
        self.breach_log: list[BreachEvent] = []
        self._lock = threading.Lock()

        # Prometheus metrics
        labels = ["vendor"]
        self.latency_hist = Histogram(
            "llm_request_latency_seconds",
            "LLM API request latency",
            labels,
            buckets=[0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 5.0, 10.0, 30.0],
        )
        self.error_counter = Counter(
            "llm_request_errors_total",
            "LLM API errors by type",
            labels + ["error_type"],
        )
        self.uptime_gauge = Gauge("llm_vendor_uptime_30d_pct", "Rolling 30d uptime %", labels)
        self.p95_gauge = Gauge("llm_latency_p95_ms", "p95 latency over last 1h", labels)
        self.p99_gauge = Gauge("llm_latency_p99_ms", "p99 latency over last 1h", labels)
        self.credit_gauge = Gauge("llm_sla_credits_usd", "Accumulated SLA credits USD", labels)
        self.breach_active = Gauge("llm_sla_breach_active", "1 if breach active", labels + ["breach_type"])

    def record_request(self, latency_ms: float, http_status: int, timed_out: bool = False):
        latency_sec = latency_ms / 1000.0
        self.latency_hist.labels(vendor=self.v).observe(latency_sec)
        with self._lock:
            self._total_requests += 1
            self._latency_window.append((time.time(), latency_ms))
            if timed_out:
                self._error_counts["timeout"] += 1
                self.error_counter.labels(vendor=self.v, error_type="timeout").inc()
            elif http_status == 429:
                self._error_counts["429"] += 1
                self.error_counter.labels(vendor=self.v, error_type="rate_limit").inc()
            elif 400 <= http_status < 500:
                self._error_counts["4xx"] += 1
                self.error_counter.labels(vendor=self.v, error_type="4xx").inc()
            elif http_status >= 500:
                self._error_counts["5xx"] += 1
                self.error_counter.labels(vendor=self.v, error_type="5xx").inc()
        self._update_derived_metrics(latency_ms, http_status)

    def _update_derived_metrics(self, latest_latency_ms: float, latest_status: int):
        now = time.time()
        one_hour_ago = now - 3600
        with self._lock:
            recent = [ms for ts, ms in self._latency_window if ts >= one_hour_ago]
        if len(recent) >= 10:
            sorted_lat = sorted(recent)
            n = len(sorted_lat)
            p95 = sorted_lat[int(n * 0.95)]
            p99 = sorted_lat[int(n * 0.99)]
            self.p95_gauge.labels(vendor=self.v).set(p95)
            self.p99_gauge.labels(vendor=self.v).set(p99)
            self._check_latency_breach("latency_p95", p95, self.config.p95_latency_sla_ms)
            self._check_latency_breach("latency_p99", p99, self.config.p99_latency_sla_ms)
        error_rate = (self._error_counts["5xx"] + self._error_counts["timeout"]) / max(self._total_requests, 1)
        self._check_latency_breach("error_rate", error_rate * 1000, self.config.error_rate_sla * 1000)

    def _check_latency_breach(self, breach_type: str, current_value: float, threshold: float):
        now = time.time()
        if current_value > threshold:
            if breach_type not in self._breach_candidates or self._breach_candidates[breach_type] is None:
                self._breach_candidates[breach_type] = now
            elif now - self._breach_candidates[breach_type] >= self.config.breach_confirm_window_sec:
                if breach_type not in self._active_breaches:
                    evt = BreachEvent(
                        vendor=self.v,
                        breach_type=breach_type,
                        start_ts=datetime.fromtimestamp(self._breach_candidates[breach_type], tz=timezone.utc),
                    )
                    self._active_breaches[breach_type] = evt
                    self.breach_active.labels(vendor=self.v, breach_type=breach_type).set(1)
        else:
            if breach_type in self._active_breaches:
                evt = self._active_breaches.pop(breach_type)
                evt.end_ts = datetime.now(tz=timezone.utc)
                duration_hours = (evt.end_ts - evt.start_ts).total_seconds() / 3600
                evt.credit_usd = self.config.monthly_spend_usd * self.config.credit_pct_per_breach * min(duration_hours / 24, 1.0)
                self.breach_log.append(evt)
                self.credit_gauge.labels(vendor=self.v).inc(evt.credit_usd)
                self.breach_active.labels(vendor=self.v, breach_type=breach_type).set(0)
            self._breach_candidates[breach_type] = None

    def total_credits_usd(self) -> float:
        return sum(e.credit_usd for e in self.breach_log)

The Prometheus alert rules below define the critical alert conditions for SLA breach detection. These fire when measured values cross SLA thresholds and have been sustained for the confirmation window, minimizing false positives while keeping detection latency below 10 minutes.

# prometheus/alerts/llm_sla_alerts.yaml
groups:
  - name: llm_sla_breaches
    interval: 60s
    rules:

      # P95 latency SLA breach (2000ms threshold)
      - alert: LLMVendorP95LatencyBreached
        expr: llm_latency_p95_ms > 2000
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: warning
          sla_dimension: latency_p95
        annotations:
          summary: "{{ $labels.vendor }} p95 latency SLA breached"
          description: "p95 latency is {{ $value | humanize }}ms, exceeds 2000ms SLA for 5+ minutes."
          runbook_url: "https://wiki.internal/llm-sla-breach-runbook"

      # P99 latency SLA breach (5000ms threshold)
      - alert: LLMVendorP99LatencyBreached
        expr: llm_latency_p99_ms > 5000
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: critical
          sla_dimension: latency_p99
        annotations:
          summary: "{{ $labels.vendor }} p99 latency SLA breached"
          description: "p99 latency is {{ $value | humanize }}ms, exceeds 5000ms SLA for 5+ minutes."

      # Error rate SLA breach (0.1% threshold on 5xx + timeout)
      - alert: LLMVendorErrorRateBreached
        expr: |
          rate(llm_request_errors_total{error_type=~"5xx|timeout"}[10m])
          /
          rate(llm_request_latency_seconds_count[10m]) > 0.001
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: critical
          sla_dimension: error_rate
        annotations:
          summary: "{{ $labels.vendor }} error rate SLA breached"
          description: "Error rate {{ $value | humanizePercentage }} exceeds 0.1% SLA."

      # Uptime SLA warning (rolling 30d approaching threshold)
      - alert: LLMVendorUptimeApproachingBreached
        expr: llm_vendor_uptime_30d_pct < 99.95
        for: 10m
        labels:
          severity: warning
          sla_dimension: uptime
        annotations:
          summary: "{{ $labels.vendor }} 30d uptime approaching SLA threshold"
          description: "30d uptime is {{ $value }}%, approaching 99.9% SLA minimum."

      # Uptime SLA breach (confirmed below 99.9%)
      - alert: LLMVendorUptimeSLABreached
        expr: llm_vendor_uptime_30d_pct < 99.9
        for: 15m
        labels:
          severity: critical
          sla_dimension: uptime
        annotations:
          summary: "{{ $labels.vendor }} monthly uptime SLA BREACHED"
          description: "30d uptime is {{ $value }}%, below contractual 99.9% minimum. File credit claim."

      # Active breach summary for SLA credit tracking
      - alert: LLMSLABreachActive
        expr: llm_sla_breach_active == 1
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: page
        annotations:
          summary: "Active SLA breach: {{ $labels.vendor }} / {{ $labels.breach_type }}"
          description: "Breach has been active for {{ $value | humanizeDuration }}. Collect evidence now."

  # Recording rules for efficient dashboard queries
  - name: llm_sla_recording
    interval: 60s
    rules:
      - record: job:llm_request_error_rate:rate10m
        expr: |
          rate(llm_request_errors_total{error_type=~"5xx|timeout"}[10m])
          /
          rate(llm_request_latency_seconds_count[10m])

      - record: job:llm_p95_latency_ms:1h
        expr: histogram_quantile(0.95, rate(llm_request_latency_seconds_bucket[1h])) * 1000

      - record: job:llm_p99_latency_ms:1h
        expr: histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(llm_request_latency_seconds_bucket[1h])) * 1000
Examples

Consider a multi-vendor setup where a company uses both OpenAI (GPT-4o for customer-facing queries) and Anthropic (Claude for internal document processing). Each vendor has distinct SLA terms, and the monitoring system must track them independently while providing a unified cross-vendor view.

The synthetic probe implementation for this scenario fires every 60 seconds per vendor, using a short standardized prompt ("Summarize the following in one sentence: The sky is blue.") that completes quickly and produces a predictable response length. The probe records separate TTFT (time-to-first-token) and total latency measurements for streaming responses, and additionally validates that the response length is above a minimum threshold—catching partial response failures that would not be detected by status code alone.

import time
import httpx
from monitor import LLMSLAMonitor, SLAConfig

openai_monitor = LLMSLAMonitor(SLAConfig(
    vendor="openai",
    uptime_sla=99.9,
    p95_latency_sla_ms=2000,
    p99_latency_sla_ms=5000,
    error_rate_sla=0.001,
    monthly_spend_usd=150_000,
    credit_pct_per_breach=0.10,
))

anthropic_monitor = LLMSLAMonitor(SLAConfig(
    vendor="anthropic",
    uptime_sla=99.9,
    p95_latency_sla_ms=2500,  # Anthropic SLA allows slightly higher
    p99_latency_sla_ms=6000,
    error_rate_sla=0.001,
    monthly_spend_usd=80_000,
    credit_pct_per_breach=0.10,
))

PROBE_PROMPT = "Summarize in one sentence: The sky appears blue due to Rayleigh scattering."

def run_probe(vendor: str, api_url: str, api_key: str, monitor: LLMSLAMonitor):
    payload = {
        "model": "gpt-4o-mini" if vendor == "openai" else "claude-haiku-20240307",
        "max_tokens": 60,
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": PROBE_PROMPT}],
    }
    start = time.perf_counter()
    http_status = 200
    timed_out = False
    try:
        response = httpx.post(
            api_url,
            json=payload,
            headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {api_key}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
            timeout=30.0,
        )
        http_status = response.status_code
        latency_ms = (time.perf_counter() - start) * 1000
        monitor.record_request(latency_ms, http_status)
    except httpx.TimeoutException:
        latency_ms = 30_000
        monitor.record_request(latency_ms, 504, timed_out=True)

# Run probes every 60s in separate threads
import threading, schedule
schedule.every(60).seconds.do(lambda: run_probe("openai", "https://api.openai.com/v1/chat/completions", OPENAI_KEY, openai_monitor))
schedule.every(60).seconds.do(lambda: run_probe("anthropic", "https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages", ANTHROPIC_KEY, anthropic_monitor))

A multi-vendor normalized score can be computed by combining all SLA dimensions into a single 0–100 score for each vendor, enabling apples-to-apples comparison on the dashboard. The score weights uptime compliance at 40%, p95 latency compliance at 30%, p99 latency compliance at 20%, and error rate compliance at 10%—reflecting the relative business impact of each dimension. Vendors that are fully compliant on all dimensions score 100; each point below represents a degradation in normalized service quality.
Real-World Example

Meridian Capital Partners, a mid-sized fintech operating AI-powered document analysis for loan underwriting, deployed LLM SLA monitoring in early 2023 after discovering—accidentally, during a quarterly invoice review—that they had experienced two separate multi-hour outages in the prior quarter that qualified for SLA credits under their enterprise contract, but had missed the 30-day claim filing deadline.

The loss that prompted action: $340,000 in unclaimed credits from six months of undocumented breaches. The firm's LLM spend was approximately $180,000 per month across two vendors, and their enterprise SLA provided 15% monthly service credits for any calendar month in which uptime fell below 99.9%. Over six months, the combined vendors had experienced four breach months—but without monitoring, Meridian had no timestamped evidence and missed every filing deadline.

The engineering team built a monitoring system over six weeks using Prometheus, Grafana, and a custom Python SLA engine. Synthetic probes fired every 60 seconds from three AWS regions (us-east-1, eu-west-1, ap-southeast-1) to detect regional vs. global outages. A real-traffic interceptor sampled 15% of production document-processing requests. The breach detection engine implemented a 5-minute confirmation window with automatic credit calculation using their exact contract formula.

Over the following 18 months, Meridian's monitoring system detected 23 distinct SLA breaches across their two vendors: 14 uptime breaches, 6 p95 latency breaches, and 3 error rate breaches. Each breach generated an automated evidence package—timestamped probe logs, metric exports, and a pre-filled credit claim form in the vendor's required format. The team filed claims within 48 hours of each breach end. Total credits recovered: $1.24 million. The monitoring system cost approximately $35,000 per year to operate (Prometheus/Grafana infrastructure, engineer time), yielding an ROI of roughly 34x in the first 18 months.

Beyond credit recovery, the monitoring data transformed Meridian's vendor relationship. Armed with 18 months of performance records showing one vendor's p99 latency routinely exceeded their contractual 5,000ms threshold during business hours in Europe, Meridian renegotiated at contract renewal to add a geographic SLA tier with separate latency commitments per region—and reduced their per-token price by 8% in exchange for a longer commitment term. The monitoring system paid for itself through both direct credit recovery and improved contract terms.
Common Mistakes

Monitoring only from one location: a probe that fires from a single availability zone cannot distinguish local network issues from genuine vendor outages. Always deploy probes in at least three geographic regions and require multiple regions to confirm a breach before declaring it.

Treating 4xx errors as vendor failures: 400, 401, 403, and 422 errors are almost always caused by the client (bad request format, invalid API key, malformed parameters). Counting them toward vendor SLA breach calculations produces inflated error rates that vendors will rightly reject.

Missing partial response detection: an LLM API can return HTTP 200 OK with a truncated response body that does not contain a valid completion. Monitoring only HTTP status codes misses this failure mode entirely—add minimum response length and JSON schema validation to probe assertions.

Not accounting for planned maintenance windows: vendors declare maintenance windows in their status feeds hours or days in advance. Downtime during declared maintenance is contractually excluded from SLA calculations in most agreements. Ignoring this generates spurious breach records that vendors dispute.

Filing claims without sufficient evidence: submitting a credit claim that says "we observed degraded service on date X" without attached metric exports, probe logs, and precise timestamps will be rejected by vendor SLA teams. Evidence collection must be automated and comprehensive from day one.

Using mean latency instead of percentiles: mean latency is dominated by the majority of fast requests and hides the tail behavior that SLAs actually target. Always track p50, p95, and p99 separately; mean is misleading for SLA compliance assessment.

Missing the claim filing deadline: most LLM vendor SLAs require credit claims to be filed within 30 days of the month in which the breach occurred. Without automated breach logging and claim generation, teams routinely discover breaches after the deadline has passed.

Ignoring streaming TTFT in latency SLAs: for streaming LLM APIs, time-to-first-token is often the metric users experience as responsiveness, and some SLAs have separate TTFT clauses. Measuring only total completion time misses TTFT breaches.

No monitoring infrastructure redundancy: if your Prometheus instance goes down during a vendor outage, you lose the evidence for the very event you need to claim credits for. Use remote write to a redundant store or a managed monitoring service for critical breach data.

Alert fatigue from no confirmation windows: firing alerts on every single probe failure produces so many false positives that engineers start ignoring them. A 3–5 minute confirmation window before alerting eliminates transient noise while keeping detection latency acceptable.
Best Practices

Read the contract before writing code: implement breach detection logic based on the exact definitions in the vendor's SLA document—uptime calculation period (monthly vs. quarterly), error exclusions, and maintenance window handling all vary significantly between vendors.

Store raw probe data for 18 months: rolling windows are useful for real-time alerting, but credit claims and vendor negotiations require access to the raw timestamped probe results from months ago. Keep raw event logs in durable storage (S3 or GCS) beyond the Prometheus retention period.

Implement a synthetic probe health check: your monitoring system needs to be monitored. Add a dead-man's switch that fires an alert if synthetic probes have not reported within 5 minutes, ensuring you detect monitoring outages before they cause evidence gaps during vendor incidents.

Standardize probe payloads across vendors: use identical prompt lengths and max_token values when comparing latency across vendors, so you are measuring comparable workloads. Differences in prompt or output length confound cross-vendor comparisons.

Automate breach evidence packaging: when a breach ends, automatically generate a PDF or JSON evidence package containing the breach timeline, metric plots, probe log excerpts, and the computed credit amount—ready to attach to a vendor support ticket.

Separate monitoring and production infrastructure: deploy the monitoring system in a separate cloud account or VPC from production to ensure a vendor outage cannot simultaneously affect both your application and your evidence collection.

Establish a monthly SLA review cadence: even without active breaches, review monthly uptime and latency trends with both the infrastructure team and procurement to catch gradual degradation before it becomes a contractual breach.

Track rate limit errors separately: 429 errors are often a signal of your own traffic patterns hitting contracted throughput limits rather than vendor failure. Separate tracking helps you distinguish vendor-caused throttling (which may be SLA-relevant) from self-inflicted rate limiting.

Use histogram metrics (not summaries) in Prometheus: Prometheus Summary type computes quantiles client-side and cannot be aggregated across instances. Histogram type supports server-side quantile computation and can be aggregated across multiple probe instances.

Integrate breach notifications with your incident management system: SLA breaches should open incidents in PagerDuty or OpsGenie with standardized severity levels so they appear in post-mortem analyses and can be correlated with user-facing incidents.
Performance and Scalability

Synthetic probe overhead is minimal compared to production traffic but must be sized appropriately. A single probe container running every 60 seconds per vendor consumes negligible CPU and memory—under 50 MB RAM and less than 1% of a single vCPU. However, probe frequency has a direct impact on breach detection latency: 60-second intervals with a 5-minute confirmation window yield a worst-case detection latency of 11 minutes (one missed probe at the start of an outage, then 5 minutes of confirmation). If your business requires faster detection, 30-second probe intervals halve the latency cost, but double the API expense.

Real-traffic instrumentation performance depends on the sampling rate and the async implementation. An in-process interceptor that calls back synchronously into a metric recording function adds 0.1–0.5 ms of overhead per request—acceptable for most LLM use cases where total latency is measured in hundreds of milliseconds. The interceptor should write to a local ring buffer and flush asynchronously to avoid any tail latency impact on the hot path. At 10% sampling of 1,000 requests per minute, the metric stream is 100 events per minute—trivially handled by any time-series database.

Prometheus scaling considerations arise when monitoring many vendors or very high request volumes. The key concern is cardinality: every unique combination of label values creates a separate time series. Avoid using highly variable labels (request IDs, user IDs, specific error messages) as Prometheus labels—this causes cardinality explosions that degrade query performance. Keep labels to high-cardinality-controlled dimensions: vendor, region, model, error_type. With five vendors and three regions each, the label cardinality remains manageable at under 100 active series per metric.

Long-term storage for SLA reporting uses Thanos or Victoria Metrics in downsampling mode: raw 15-second resolution is retained for 7 days, 1-minute aggregates for 90 days, and 5-minute aggregates for 2 years. This three-tier retention scheme keeps storage costs under $50/month for a typical monitoring deployment while preserving the granularity needed for recent incident investigation and the historical depth needed for annual vendor reviews.

Dashboard query performance requires recording rules to avoid expensive real-time aggregations on the presentation layer. Pre-computing rolling p95/p99 latency, 30-day uptime percentage, and error rates as recording rules means Grafana dashboard queries resolve in under 100 ms even when the underlying data spans 18 months. Without recording rules, queries over long time ranges can take 10–30 seconds, making dashboards practically unusable.
Tooling and Ecosystem

The LLM SLA monitoring ecosystem draws from both the traditional observability stack and emerging LLM-specific tools. The core infrastructure choices are time-series storage, alerting, and visualization.

Time-series databases: Prometheus is the most widely used option for this workload—its pull-based model works naturally with probe exporters, its label system handles multi-vendor scenarios cleanly, and its ecosystem of Grafana dashboards and alert manager integrations is mature. InfluxDB is an alternative with native support for high-precision timestamps and a SQL-like query language (Flux) that some teams find more intuitive for percentile calculations. Victoria Metrics is a drop-in Prometheus replacement that offers better write throughput and lower resource consumption for large-scale deployments.
Prometheus + Grafana: the standard open-source stack; excellent ecosystem, self-hosted, no vendor lock-in. Best for teams with existing Kubernetes infrastructure.

Datadog: managed observability platform with native LLM monitoring capabilities in Datadog LLM Observability, including token usage tracking, cost attribution, and latency percentiles across vendors. Higher cost but zero operational overhead.

New Relic: provides AI monitoring features in New Relic AI, with vendor SLA dashboards, alert policies, and automated anomaly detection. Integrates tightly with APM for correlating vendor latency with application response times.

Helicone: open-source LLM observability proxy that intercepts API calls and records latency, cost, and error data with no code changes. Offers a hosted dashboard and alerting tier. Best for teams that want monitoring without writing instrumentation code.

LangFuse: open-source LLM observability platform focused on traces, evals, and dataset management. Includes API latency tracking but is primarily a product analytics tool rather than an SLA compliance tool.

Cronitor and StatusCake: synthetic monitoring services that can probe LLM API endpoints on a schedule without requiring self-hosted infrastructure. Limited in LLM-specific metrics but useful for basic uptime tracking with minimal setup.

Thanos and Cortex: Prometheus long-term storage backends that add global query federation across multiple Prometheus instances, object storage backing (S3/GCS), and 18-month+ retention—essential for annual SLA reporting.

PagerDuty and OpsGenie: alert routing and on-call management systems that receive breach alerts from Prometheus Alertmanager and route them to the appropriate engineering team with escalation policies.

Betterstack (formerly Uptime): managed uptime monitoring service with a free tier suitable for initial LLM endpoint monitoring. Provides status page integration to cross-reference your measurements with vendor-published availability.
Comparison with Alternatives

Organizations have three primary approaches to LLM vendor SLA monitoring: building a self-hosted system (as described in this document), relying on the vendor's own dashboards, or subscribing to a third-party managed monitoring platform. Each has distinct trade-offs across cost, control, coverage, and operational complexity.

The hybrid approach used by most mature enterprises combines a managed platform (Datadog or New Relic) for operational alerting and incident correlation with a self-built credit tracking module that queries the managed platform's API to extract breach evidence and compute claims. This retains the operational simplicity of managed infrastructure while adding the contract-specific credit calculation logic that no generic platform provides out of the box.
Security and Reliability

The monitoring system itself must be more reliable than the systems it monitors. If the monitoring infrastructure goes down during a vendor outage, you lose both the ability to detect the outage quickly and the evidence needed to claim credits for it. This requires treating the monitoring stack as critical infrastructure with its own availability requirements.

Probe agent redundancy is the first reliability requirement. Deploy probe agents in at least three geographically separated availability zones, and require that at least two of three zones confirm a failure before declaring an outage. This prevents a single-AZ network event from triggering false breach declarations. For multi-cloud environments, run probes from both AWS and GCP to provide maximum independence from any single cloud provider's network path to the vendor.

API key security for monitoring probes requires careful credential management. Probe agents need their own dedicated API keys with the minimum required permissions—typically just the ability to make completion API calls. These keys should be rotated quarterly and stored in a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never embed API keys in probe container images or configuration files checked into source control. A compromised probe key could allow an attacker to consume your API quota or extract information about your prompt patterns.

Metric storage reliability requires remote write redundancy. Prometheus by default stores metrics only on local disk, meaning a single instance failure loses recent data. Configure Prometheus to remote write to both a local Thanos sidecar and a cloud-hosted backup (Grafana Cloud's free tier or AWS Managed Grafana) to ensure breach evidence is never lost due to a monitoring infrastructure failure. For breach events specifically, write breach records to a durable PostgreSQL database with regular backups—these records are financial evidence and must be retained with the same care as accounting records.
Use dedicated API keys for monitoring probes, separate from application keys, to allow independent rotation and access control.
Encrypt metrics storage at rest, especially if probe responses include any content that could reveal your prompt patterns or use-case details.
Restrict dashboard access with RBAC—SLA credit data and vendor performance records may be commercially sensitive; limit visibility to appropriate roles.
Implement a dead-man's switch (a separate monitor that confirms your probes are running) to detect monitoring system failures before they create evidence gaps.
Retain breach event logs for at least 36 months—vendor disputes can surface years after the breach if contract terms include multi-year audit windows.
Standardize probe request content to avoid accidentally including PII or confidential data in monitoring probes—use synthetic prompt text, not real user data.
Mental Model

Think of LLM vendor SLA monitoring as running an independent quality assurance department for your suppliers. Just as a manufacturer receiving parts from a supplier does not simply trust the supplier's own quality reports—but instead runs incoming inspection with independent measurement tools against the contractual specification—your monitoring system provides independent, continuous incoming inspection of the LLM API service your vendor delivers.

The SLA itself is the specification: it defines acceptable uptime, latency, and error rate the same way a manufacturing specification defines acceptable dimensions and tolerances. Synthetic probes are your measurement instruments: they produce calibrated, repeatable measurements independent of production variation. The breach detection engine is your statistical process control system: it monitors whether the process is in control and raises an alert when it drifts outside specification. The credit tracking system is your warranty claim management system: it documents when defective service was delivered and computes what you are owed.

A second useful framing is the separation between observed reality and contractual agreement. Vendors publish status pages and sometimes claim their uptime is higher than your measurements show—because they measure at their network edge while you experience latency through multiple network hops. The SLA contract typically specifies where measurement occurs; if it does not specify, your monitoring data (measured from your infrastructure) is the relevant evidence. This framing helps when having disputes with vendors: the conversation is about measurement methodology, not about whose numbers are right.

Finally, think about time horizons. Real-time monitoring serves operational needs—detect and respond to outages fast. Rolling 24-hour and 7-day metrics serve tactical optimization—identify patterns that inform routing decisions. Rolling 30-day metrics serve contractual compliance—compute monthly SLA compliance for credit claims. Trailing 12-month metrics serve strategic decisions—inform contract renewal negotiations and vendor consolidation strategy. Each time horizon requires different data granularity and retention, and a good SLA monitoring architecture preserves all four.
Key Takeaways
SLA monitoring is how you turn a vendor contract from a document into an enforceable performance commitment—without continuous measurement, SLA guarantees are unverifiable and uncollectable.
The four core dimensions to track are uptime (rolling 30-day %), latency percentiles (p50/p95/p99), error rate (5xx + timeout fraction), and throughput (tokens per minute vs. committed tier).
Synthetic probes (scheduled every 60 seconds from multiple regions) and real-traffic sampling (10–20% of production requests) are complementary—neither alone provides sufficient coverage.
Breach detection requires a confirmation window (3–5 minutes of sustained threshold violation) to eliminate false positives without adding unacceptable detection latency.
Credit calculation must implement the exact formula from the contract—most enterprise LLM SLAs specify tiered credits (10–30% of monthly spend) based on the severity and duration of the breach.
Prometheus + Grafana is the industry-standard open-source stack for this workload; Thanos or Victoria Metrics extends retention to the 18+ months needed for contract negotiations and annual reports.
The 30-day claim filing deadline is the single most common reason enterprises fail to recover owed credits—automated breach detection and claim generation is the only reliable solution at scale.

Vendor dashboards measure from the vendor's infrastructure edge, while your monitoring measures end-to-end from your environment—this difference in measurement point is legitimate and should be documented in your contract.
The monitoring system itself needs redundancy—deploy probe agents in multiple AZs, remote-write metrics to durable storage, and monitor the monitors with a dead-man's switch.

At $180,000/month in LLM API spend, even a single breach month recovering 15% credits yields $27,000—the ROI of a well-built monitoring system is typically realized within the first quarter of operation.
SLA monitoring data compounds in value over time: 18 months of performance history is the most powerful asset you can bring to a vendor contract renewal negotiation.
